Document: Salusbury Arms Tremeirchion   Tremeirchion Community Pub Ltd The pub is a vital part of any community, and within the village of Tremeirchion the community were given a window of opportunity to purchase the building and its grounds. The Tremeirchion community along with neighbouring villages surrounding expressed their desire to support this much needed community hub. The Tremeirchion Community Pub Limited successfully raised a total of £195,800 through share investments in July 2023, along with successfully being awarded £175,000 as a capital grant from the UK Governments Community Ownership Fund. This enabled for the purchase the Salusbury Arms and to re-open as a Community Owned Pub on August 18th 2023. We will continue to post updates in the Latest News section of this webpage, but more regularly on social media (You can find us on Facebook, Instagram and view the pubs webpage here: www.SalusburyArms.co.uk) A huge thank you to all that have pulled together so far to save our pub! This project is in its infancy, and we hope you join us on this exciting journey!

Document: Hartcliffe City Farm   Case study £300,000 re-opens Hartcliffe City Farm Hartcliffe City Farm closed its doors to the public in 2021. It is now back up and running after receiving £300,000 from the Community Ownership Fund. The investment will help secure the future of the farm in South Bristol. From: Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities Published 17 January 2023 A thriving community hub Windmill City Farm is the organisation leading the development. This is in partnership with South Bristol charity nicknamed the ‘Heart of BS13’. The organisation aims to turn the farm into a community hub with: open space for outdoor learning gardens and ponds access to animals Fresh local vegetables are also grown on the site and sold to local people at the farm café or in boxes. This provides a source of income for the site. Community Ownership Fund investment The funding will support vital development of the farm’s infrastructure. This will include: ensuring that the farm is accessible to all plumbing wiring Find out more about levelling up. Published 17 January 2023   The long road to making Hartcliffe City Farm fabulous again Four years ago, the farm in one of Bristol's most deprived areas was mired in council controversy and collapse and closed for two years. Now, the dream of a modern, family-friendly free farm venue is about to be fulfilled What's On Tristan Cork Chief Reporter 05:00, 29 Sep 2024 Year 2 children from Merchants Academy in Withywood help with the harvest at Hartcliffe City Farm in October 2023 - A new Playful Barn is being launched at this year's Hartcliffe City Farm's Harvest Festival event on October 19, which is a culmination of three years of hard work by volunteers and staff from Windmill Hill City Farm and the Heart of BS13 community group to turn the farm around after it closed in 2020.(Image: Hartcliffe City Farm) Three years of hard work by a team of community volunteers and local organisations have turned the dream of bringing a city farm in South Bristol back to life into a reality. The final part of the work to create a vibrant new city farm in Hartcliffe will be launched next month, with the grand opening of a ‘Playful Barn’, providing an indoor venue that will be free and available all year round for local families. The launch will take place at a big Harvest Festival event at Hartcliffe City Farm, which is happening on Saturday, October 19, with the party between 10.30am and 2.30am, and the farm itself open as usual until 4pm. It’s been a long road for Hartcliffe City Farm, which was set up by the South Bristol community back in the 1970s, after the controversy and collapse of the previous organisation that ran it, in 2019 and the months leading up to the Covid pandemic in early 2020. Town 2 hours from Bristol dubbed 'Notting-Hill-on-Sea' but homes are much cheaper Lonely Mouth cafe in Bristol hit with shock £34k bill Bristol City Council took back control of the farm, and it remained closed for two years as a number of takeovers and rescue plans were proposed, before the council handed it to a partnership between local community group Heart of BS13, and the expertise of Windmill Hill City Farm in Bedminster. A Spring Fair shearing event at Hartcliffe City Farm in 2024. Until now, events like this have had to take place under temporary gazebos, but now a new Playful Barn is being launched at Hartcliffe City Farm's Harvest Festival event on October 19 A Spring Fair shearing event at Hartcliffe City Farm. Until now, events like this have had to take place under temporary gazebos, but now a new Playful Barn is being launched at Hartcliffe City Farm's Harvest Festival event on October 19(Image: Hartcliffe City Farm) Get the latest updates for families plus selected offers and competitions Enter your Email... Subscribe We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and third parties based on our knowledge of you. More info They took on the farm in 2021, and it finally reopened with a Jubilee Party in 2022, albeit with ambitious plans to expand, enhance, build and refurbish the site completely. “It’s been three years of really hard graft to sort the place out, get it back up and running and then start building and improving, and the Playful Barn is the big part of that, the final piece,” said Ellie Vowles, who is part of the team. “The team have done lots of groundwork and we’re now in a position where the farm is its own organisation, still obviously with our parents being Windmill Hill and Heart of BS13. A sneak peak at the new Playful Barn, which is being launched at Hartcliffe City Farm's Harvest Festival event on October 19(Image: Hartcliffe City Farm) “This is a very different site now, and we’ve such amazing community support from everyone in Hartcliffe, who have continued to back us, even though this has been a building site for a quite a while,” she added. The farm’s pigs, goats, sheep and lambs, chickens, geese, ducks and guinea pigs are firm favourites with local children and parents, and the Heart of BS13 run a separate Flower Farm on part of the site, that fills the area with colour. The farm itself is very visible, but not routinely open to the public - apart from on event days like the Harvest Festival. “What’s been missing is a play space that’s free for the children and is there for when it rains, indoors, that can be the heart of the farm, and the barn conversion, securing the roof and all the rest of the work has been great,” added Ellie. Maypole Dancing at Hartcliffe City Farm - A new Playful Barn is being launched at Hartcliffe City Farm's Harvest Festival event on October 19(Image: Hartcliffe City Farm) “The Playful Barn has everything. We’ve got a cafe area with picnic tables, a big mud kitchen for children to play in, a wobbly bridge, climbing stuff, a tree house, and a reading corner, and there’s play equipment for the toddlers, too. We’re really looking forward to finishing it all and opening it up so it can be something free for all the local families right through the winter,” said Ellie. The Harvest Festival event is £1 to enter, with the option to donate to the farm with a £3 ticket if you can afford it, while under-11s are free. Tickets are available on the door and on the Eventbrite site, and the farm says tickets for children have to be booked to give them an idea of how many people are coming.

Document: The Gunny, Canvey Island The Gunny Project, Canvey Island Canvey Island, Essex, United Kingdom £3,460 Successful 18 supporters Share Need to raise money? Get started with Crowdfunder Matt King profile image Matt King About the project owner Aim: We're transforming a neglected site for people & nature: developing a community hub, therapeutic garden, wildlife area, play areas & trees The Gunny Project: Connecting Communities, Mental Health Support, Getting Physically Active and Environmental Conservation Trust Links are working with Canvey Island Big Local to revive The Gunny, a neglected 3-acre former military site in the heart of Canvey Island, back into a thriving community space, and we need your help. 1696018288_tl_gunny_project_tote_bag_illustration_rv2.jpg Who are Trust Links? Trust Links is a local independent charity aiming to improve mental health, wellbeing and the environment in Essex. We run 6 Growing Together community gardens in mid and south Essex and are excited to develop a new project on Canvey Island working with local partners. Who are Canvey Island Big Local? Canvey Island Big Local is a partnership of local residents, who are part of the Local Trust Big Local Programme funded through The National Lottery Community Fund. What are our plans? We have big plans for The Gunny! With support from the wider community and businesses, we can make our plans a reality. The Gunny will provide a range of facilities to the community including: A community building: for the community to meet, socialise, enjoy workshops, activities and access local support services. Play areas: For children and young people to play and have fun. Outdoor gym facilities: For all ages to benefit from physical activity. Picnic benches, raised vegetable beds, pergola, accessible paths: Safe spaces to connect with others, relax, and enjoy the benefits of nature. Environmental conservation: a biodiverse space, with a variety of trees, shrubs, natural wetland area and wildflower meadow. Growing Together therapeutic gardening project: to support with mental wellbeing, building on successful Trust Links projects across Essex. Local people will get active through the outdoor gym, play area, and structured gardening activities in this wonderful new space! Your 1696273618_gunny-1_(3).jpgdonation will help provide a valuable, beneficial green space Picture: What The Gunny looks like at the moment You can support to help provide a vibrant, green, biodiverse community space for years to come. 1695032407_eco_(facebook_cover).pngAt Trust Links we believe in taking care of the environment because it takes care of us. With donations received through Crowdfunder, we can provide a range of features beneficial to residents of Canvey Island AND to the environment. In the garden area this includes: vegetable growing, beehives, planting trees and shrubs, bug hotels, willow dome, developing a wetland area and a wildflower meadow! We are hoping the new community will include solar panels, rainwater harvesting, and air source heating. 3D plans for the Growing Together area of The Gunny 1695205799_3d_gunny_2.jpg Above image: 3D Plans of the Growing Together section of The Gunny This beautiful, biodiverse green space will provide a tranquil backdrop for the Growing Together gardens that will be available on The Gunny site, providing therapeutic gardening and social activities to support local people with their mental health and wellbeing. The Gunny Project will transform lives and be a valuable asset to Canvey Island. This project is also helping people become more active! Not only will we be encouraging people to walk and cycle to the site, but people will enjoy physical activity when they get to The Gunny. Our outdoor gym equipment for teenagers and adults will include a rower, push hands, lat pull and chest presses, air walkers, trinity cycles and long trampolines! Gardening can be a great physical work out too, with digging, pruning, mowing and clearing all increasing anaerobic exercise and strengthening muscles. As we recover from the COVID-19 pandemic, we need to build our resilience. We are reinventing a neglected space to become a place for positive activity, wellbeing and building community. Can you help make our dream a reality? 1696272651_thundersley_party.jpg Picture: Afternoon Tea Party at Trust Links Thundersley The Gunny Project is located in an area of multiple deprivation, with higher than average levels of inactivity and obesity. Trust Links and our partners will be running programmes for adults with mental health problems and learning disabilities, young people, and everyone in the community, helping to increase active levels. The gardening activities, outdoor gym and play will have a great impact on the health and wellbeing of local people, as it will be a freely accessible resource to increase exercise levels. There will be structured activities and community events, so everyone can get involved and join in! Canvey Island Youth Project will be putting on activities for young people to get them ac1696272288_westcliff_1.jpgtive and involved too. Picture: Gardening at Growing Together Westcliff The brand new community building will host a range of activities and services including a men’s shed project, social groups, and peer support groups. The building will also be available for other people to use so it could be used for pilates, yoga, seated exercises or similar can be set up there, once it is established. Your donation will help pay for trees, shrubs, vegetable growing area, outdoor gym equipment, and the building of accessible paths around the site. All donations are match funded, meaning that your donation will have a greater impact! Thank you for your support! 1696273251_orchard_apple_day.jpgPicture: Apple Day run by Trust Links at St Laurence Orchard, Eastwood Trust Links and Canvey Island Big Local are transforming the Gunny! Giorgia01 September 2023 Trust Links and the Canvey Big local at the Gunny site img Trust Links are working with Canvey Island Big Local to revive The Gunny, a former military site in the heart of Canvey Island, into a thriving community space, coming 2025. After 10 years of work by the Canvey Island Big Local and local partners, we are thrilled to announce the launch of building works commencing at the historic Gunny site. The building works launch event took place on Friday 29th November where we invited the public to join in celebrating the beginning of the building works which will turn the empty, historic site into a thriving community asset. Check out the video below from our Building works launch event! What’s coming to the Gunny? There are ambitious plans for The Gunny, and with the support of the wider community and local businesses, the plans are set to create a vibrant hub that will benefit residents of all ages. The exciting vision includes: A community building: A welcoming space for the community to enjoy courses, workshops, social groups, meet-ups and much more! Growing Together therapeutic gardening project to support with mental health and wellbeing: An addition to our already established and successful therapeutic gardening projects in Westcliff, Shoeburyness, Thundersley, Rochford, Basildon and Braintree Play area: For children and young people to play and have fun Outdoor gym facilities: For all ages to benefit from physical activity Picnic benches, raised vegetable beds, pergola, accessible paths: Safe spaces to connect with others, relax, and enjoy the benefits of nature. Pergolas Accessible paths that provide safe spaces for relaxation and connection with nature Environmental conservation: The Gunny will become a biodiverse space including trees, wetland area and wildflower meadow View The Gunny Plan 1 View The Gunny Plan 2 How YOU can support The Gunny project There are a number of ways you can support the transformation of this valuable community asset now and for future generations: Volunteer for tree planting, fundraising, and promotional opportunities available. To book onto a free tree planting workshop, click here! Spread the Word! Share news about the Gunny through social media, groups, and your community connections. Organise a fundraising event. Whether it’s a bake sale, coffee morning or golf day; any money raised will be gratefully received and will help! Sponsor the Gunny. There are a range of levels available for sponsorship. If you or your business would like to get involved, contact our team through the information at the bottom of this page, and view our Sponsorship options here. Gifts in Kind. Can you provide a gift such as flowers or resources for the gardens? We’d love to hear from you! What is the Gunny? During WWII The Gunny was an Anti-Aircraft Gun Battery and barracks, supporting the defence of London. In the 1960s The Gunny was turned into a park in the 1970s one of the first skate parks in the UK was built on the site. Over the years lack of funding meant the skate part gradually fell into ruin. By the 2000s the site was a muddy marsh and today is mostly used by dog walkers. We plan to transform The Gunny into a safe space for the local community. Once completed in 2025, there will be opportunities for people of all ages to connect, engage with nature, and enjoy activities in the hub and community garden. If you would like to talk to our team about supporting The Gunny, Canvey Island, please get in touch by emailing fundraising@trustlinks.org – we would love to hear from you! Please be aware The Gunny site will be closed while building works are taking place from now until July 2025. We will be planning a visit of the building works in April 2025 for all partners and stakeholders to see the work in progress, keep an eye on our social media and website for further information. Canvey Gunny project sees building work begin yesterday 28th January Heritage Planning and development By George Pizani Trainee Reporter @george_pizani Share 1 Comment Progress - Builders are now on-site at the Gunny (Image: Trust Links) Progress - Builders are now on-site at the Gunny (Image: Trust Links) BUILDING work has finally begun as part of a £1 million project to transform a Second World War gun battery into a bustling community space and garden. Yesterday saw the arrival of builders at Canvey’s historic Gunny site following more than a decade of planning and preparation. Progress - Builders are now on the site and beginning work Progress - Builders are now on the site and beginning work (Image: Trust Links) Having been used as an anti-aircraft gun battery and barracks during the war, and later a skatepark and recreation grounds, the Gunny is set to be given a new lease of life with the construction of a new community building, outdoor fitness area and accessible paths. Brits Can’t Get Enough of These Hand-Painted Book Mugs HikeCare | Sponsored Expansive - The Gunny is a large Canvey site with massive potential Expansive - The Gunny is a large Canvey site with massive potential (Image: Trust Links) We're now on WhatsApp! Join our new channel at to get all the latest breaking news and exclusive stories delivered straight to your phone. Movement - The Gunny site has now been secured with builders moving in their facilities Movement - The Gunny site has now been secured with builders moving in their facilities (Image: Trust Links) "Builders have moved in and have set up the facilities that will allow them to get the work done - they're getting the diggers in and breaking ground," said Matt King, CEO of Trust Links, a south Essex mental health charity spearheading the Gunny's regeneration. Ground-breaking - Builders have begun laying accessible paths at the Gunny Ground-breaking - Builders have begun laying accessible paths at the Gunny (Image: Trust Links) "It's been a very long time coming and a lot of people thought it wouldn't even happen at all." Adding: "It's fantastic to see a site with so much historical value and importance for the community being transformed into something that can continue to offer benefits to the community on Canvey." Happy - Residents celebrated the confirmation of the Gunny's redevelopment last year (Image: Trust Links) Happy - Residents celebrated the confirmation of the Gunny's redevelopment last year (Image: Trust Links) (Image: Trust Links) The project, which is expected to be completed by mid-July, will see the site turned into a multi-purpose community space and garden, with features designed to appeal to residents of all ages. Transformed - CGI images show a completely regenerated Gunny site Transformed - CGI images show a completely regenerated Gunny site (Image: Trust Links) In addition to a new community building, play area and outdoor gym, Trust Links is also working to improve bio-diversity at the site through a new wetlands area and planting beds. Nostalgia - Cllr Peter May is pleased to see the Gunny return to proper public use Nostalgia - Cllr Peter May is pleased to see the Gunny return to proper public use (Image: Trust Links) Peter May, Independent councillor for Canvey Central Ward grew up on the island and spent his youth playing on the Gunny. He has been involved with its regeneration from the project’s inception and has described the start of building works as “a long time coming”. Community - Castle Point councillors, residents and Trust Links volunteers recently participated in a tree planting at the site Community - Castle Point councillors, residents and Trust Links volunteers recently participated in a tree planting at the site (Image: Trust Links) “As a kid, that’s where we used to play. We had goal posts up there and would go newting in the little lake,” he said. “Brining it back is going to be a great thing for Canvey as it will provide a community space that everyone will be able to enjoy.” Visiting Canvey Monday 1st September 2025 at 11:00 🌿✨ Exciting news for Canvey Island! ✨🌿 The official launch of The Gunny Community Project 🎉 📍 West Crescent, Canvey Island 📅 Saturday 20th September 2025 🕛 12 noon – 3pm Enjoy a fun-filled afternoon with: 🎶 Live Music 🔥 BBQ & Refreshments 🎈 Kids Activities 🛍️ Stalls ✂️ Ribbon Cutting Ceremony …and so much more! This is a brilliant opportunity to discover everything The Gunny has to offer and celebrate a brand new community space in the heart of Canvey. 💚 👉 Save the date, bring your family & friends, and be part of something special! More info: www.trustlinks.org/gunny-project #CanveyIsland #Community #TheGunny #TrustLinks #BigLocal

Document: Westhall Racehorse Community Pub   Case study £100,000 Community Ownership funding helps save The Racehorse The Racehorse pub in Westhall, East Suffolk, has now been bought by the local community with the help of £100,000 from the Community Ownership Fund. From: Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities Published 17 January 2023 A key part of the Westhall Community The Racehorse has been part of the community since the 1860s but closed in 2016. Although the pub was leased back to the community in 2019, it continued to struggle. Support from the government allowed local villagers to purchase the pub in March 2022. The whole community are involved in the running of the pub. This includes: serving behind the bar organising quiz nights cleaning the beer pipes It is hoped the community ownership of the pub will help reenergise the village. Find out more about levelling up.   Villagers 'over the moon' to complete purchase of east Suffolk pub 23rd March 2022 Food and drink Pubs and bars By Tom Swindles Live Reporter Share 0 Comments 2 An east Suffolk pub has been bought out by villagers following a four year campaign to save it from permanent closure. The Racehorse in Westhall, near Southwold, was bought by the Race for the Racehorse campaign this week after it raised £160,000. In October last year, the campaign was selected as one of the community organisations to be awarded a grant under the government's Community Ownership Fund – enabling the purchase of the pub to be completed. Volunteers from the group applied for the government grant last summer after discovering cash would be made available in Chancellor Rishi Sunak's autumn Budget. Luminous dog - handmade lamp that makes hearts shine Luminous Dog™ | Sponsored Since reopening under community management, the Racehorse has proved popular for its Suffolk beers, well-selected wines and home-cooked food. Chris Punt, co-founder of the Race for the Racehorse campaign, said: “We needed another £96,000 to be able to buy the pub and were over the moon when we heard in October that we were one of just 21 community organisations in the UK to be awarded a grant under the government’s Community Ownership Fund. East Anglian Daily Times: The Racehorse Community Pub in Westhall has recently been purchased by the Race for the Racehorse campaign The Racehorse Community Pub in Westhall has recently been purchased by the Race for the Racehorse campaign (Image: Racehorse Community Pub) "We were really pleased to receive feedback that the level of community support and the sound business case tipped the scales in our favour.” The Racehorse had been closed for three years before being leased by the community in December 2019 – but now that the purchase has taken place, the future of the site is secured. The pub has been part of the Westhall community since about 1860 and now that the takeover is completed, it will be run by villagers. East Anglian Daily Times: Chancellor Rishi Sunak announced funding for the pub last autumn Chancellor Rishi Sunak announced funding for the pub last autumn (Image: PA Wire/PA Images) Liz Miller, a resident of Westhall for more than 30 years, said: "Running our own pub has got everyone involved, whether growing fruit and veg for the kitchen, washing the dishes, making the ever-popular sausage rolls, serving behind the bar, cleaning the beer pipes, organizing events such as community lunches, quiz nights or the popular open mic evenings, or just being on hand to chat to anyone who needs a friendly ear. "It’s been great to see everyone pulling together and I feel passionately about the value that our pub brings to the village."

Document: The Bikery   Huntly's Bikery: Charity reveals timeline for purchase and upgrade of old J&I Smith bakery after £50,000 boost By Lewis McBlane - lewis.mcblane@hnmedia.co.uk Published: 08:30, 23 December 2023 A CHARITY bike shop in Huntly has shared its plans to buy and upgrade the building it is based in, after receiving a £50,000 grant. From left: Laura McNeil, fundraising and retail support manager, Gordon Rural Action chief officer Emma Selway-Grant and Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Aberdeenshire Joanna, Lady Aberdeen at The Bikery's official opening...Picture: Beth Taylor.From left: Laura McNeil, fundraising and retail support manager, Gordon Rural Action chief officer Emma Selway-Grant and Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Aberdeenshire Joanna, Lady Aberdeen at The Bikery's official opening...Picture: Beth Taylor. The Bikery, on Gordon Street, has revealed its next steps after receiving the cash from the UK Government's Community Ownership Fund. Spearheaded by charity Gordon Rural Action (GRA), the social enterprise aims to get affordable bikes into the community while reducing the amount going to landfill. Since opening in March, what used to be the base of Huntly family bakery J&I Smith has been transformed into a kitted out shopfront and workshop. GRA chief officer Emma Selway-Grant said the charity hopes to buy the building in the early part of 2024. Also a priority is getting the building to a high standard, so it can keep being an asset to Huntly, she added. Among the improvements planned are upgrades to boost energy efficiency, as well as revamping the heating, plumbing and electrical systems – along with a purpose-built training area for budding bike mechanics. Ms Selway-Grant said: "Gordon Rural Action are delighted to have been successful in our application to the Community Ownership fund, funded by UK Government. "This funding will allow us to purchase the building housing the Bikery and the Food Pod helping to secure their future for the community of Huntly and the surrounding area. "We are very exciting to be able to move into the next stages with the Bikery project and plan further upgrades to the building in the future to improve energy efficiency including upgrades to the heating, plumbing and electrics ensuring the building can remain in use for our community for many years to come. "We hope to have the purchase finalised in the first quarter of 2024." UK Government Minister for Scotland Malcolm Offord said: "It's great news that a further 17 Scottish projects are sharing £8 million from the UK Government Community Ownership Fund. "Through the fund we are now supporting 43 community groups across Scotland to the tune of £13.6 million to breathe new life into the places where they live, work and play. “In total we are directly investing more than £2.7 billion in hundreds of projects across Scotland as we help grow our economy and level up the country." Secretary of State for Levelling Up Michael Gove said: “Investing in communities improves lives – whether that’s rebuilding a community centre, rescuing historic pubs and theatres or upgrading sports facilities used by families every day. “These are unique and cherished places, valued by local people of all ages, and we know how much they mean to communities. The Community Ownership Fund is about restoring pride in the places people call home and I’m delighted to be funding these projects.”   The Bikery Purpose The Bikery provides low-cost, recycled cycling equipment to the community, ensuring equality through access for all. Working collaboratively with Partners to maximise our impact in the community ensuring the reach and sustainability of the business. Adopting a disused building within the town to relocate and expand the original project. Renovating the building to create a new shop and workshop space within the town center; working towards the town’s regeneration plan. Our project intends to reduce the number of bikes entering landfill through our recycling service and promote a re-use message within the community. Supporting local transportation options, reducing car usage and emissions. Reducing environmental impact by re-purposing a rundown building which has stood empty since 2018 and updating it with net-zero in mind. The Bikery Services All bikes sold from The Bikery have undergone a full service and have been upgraded to ensure they are ready for your next cycling adventure. Our mechanics are all Velotech trained to at least Bronze level, our lead mechanic is Gold Velotech trained to ensure a high standard of repairs to all our bikes. The Bikery provides: Bike sales – We have bikes for sale for all age groups, if we don’t have your size available, we can take your details and contact you when we do. In 2023 we will be selling band new bikes alongside our recycled bikes. Bike servicing starting at £35 for a 12-point check (any new parts charged separately) Bike parts for repairs Volunteering Opportunities To book your bike in for a service, make a donation or to find out more please call 07572674501 Or call in Tues – Fri 10am-4pm 49 Gordon Street, Huntly

Document: Community Building for Whitley Bay   The Hub funded by levelling up_edited.jpg The hub is a community owned space that is used to bring local people together to address the priorities for Whitley Bay. It is located at 158 Whitley Road, Whitley Bay, NE26 2LY. Until 2017 it was Whitley Bay Job Centre and a lot of people still remember it as the Job Centre. ​ Whitley Bay Big Local bought the building in November 2021 with a plan to develop it into a community owned asset. Today the hub is a multi-purpose community space with a cafe, garden and meeting rooms. But it hasn't always been that. Here you can learn a little about it's history, the changes we've made, and our plans for the future The building was purchased for the local community, thanks to a grant received from the Government Department of Levelling Up and is managed by Whitley Bay Big Local. Our shared ambition is to convert the building into a fit for purpose community hub and 'near zero emissions building. We have recently completed two major projects and several others to bring the make the building work as a centre for communities. solar array.jpg The first major project was completed in January 2023. The works replaced the aged felt roof with a modern UPVC surface. We have upgraded the insulation and added a 23KWp solar array to reduce our running costs and reduce our impact on the world around us. The modern UPVC surface allows us to eventually keep plants on the roof and is recyclable at the end of it's life. The solar array produces all of the electricity used at the building, unless it's dark or raining, or covered in snow. Surplus electricty is sold back to the grid. Thanks to our funders - Department of Levelling Up, North of Tyne Combined Authority and Local Trust for their support. 20230803_133321.jpg The second major project was completed in June 2023. This project converted the ground floor of the building into a fit for purpose community hub. It created new accessible doors into the building and into the garden. A corridor was created to allow direct access to the upper floors. Accessible WCs were added to the ground floor. A kitchen/cafe was added and the garden was refurbished and tidied up. The ground floor is now a much more welcoming space and we are seeing more and more people coming in every day. Thanks to all of our funders who made this possible - Co-op Foundation, Biffa Award, Local Trust, Joicey Trust, SIB Group, North of Tyne Combined Authority. Other projects have included the replacement of a very old boiler with a modern condensing gas boiler. This has seen a reduction in heating bills of almost 40%. We have split a large room on the middle floor of the building into two, creating a large activity room and a separate room that is currently occupied by SPARCS, a local charity supporting people with Autism. We are in the process of freshening up the rooms on the top floor, ready for meetings and community activities to take place. The Hub's official relaunch will be on September 21st, at which point the hub will truly be a space for everyone. CONTACT US Whitley Bay Big Local: 158 Whitley Road, NE26 2LY 0191 2523570 ​ hello@whitleybaybiglocal.org Registered Charity: 1171848   Whitley Bay charity secures grants to move ahead with green transformation By David Sedgwick Published 12th Nov 2021, 09:00 BST Whitley Bay Big Local CEO David Carnaffan outside the charity building. Whitley Bay Big Local CEO David Carnaffan outside the charity building. This article contains affiliate links. We may earn a small commission on items purchased through this article, but that does not affect our editorial judgement. A former job centre building in Whitley Bay is set for a ‘green’ transformation after a charity received grants to buy and renovate it. Whitley Bay Big Local has been working within the building on Whitley Road for the past two years, running art and community sessions and offering a food collection point throughout lockdown. News And now, thanks to more than £450,000 of funding from the Community Ownership Fund, North of Tyne Combined Authority and Big Local programme, the charity has bought the property and will work with the community to create a low carbon ‘Eco Hub’. Advertisement Hide Ad David Carnaffan, CEO of Whitley Bay Big Local, said: "These grants will allow us to be truly ambitious in our vision for transforming the building into a space to inform and inspire local people about climate change, whilst continuing to be a welcoming space to engage local people in arts and community activities. “As someone who was brought up here in Whitley Bay, it makes me proud to be involved with this fantastic charity and to be helping it move forward in such a positive direction. “We are all looking forward to the building being an inclusive, green, community space, with something for everyone in Whitley Bay.” Whitley Bay Big Local is a community-led charity with a focus on the environment and creating activities for people across the town. Advertisement Hide Ad Its list of achievements includes the reintroduction of the Whitley Bay Carnival and development of an environmental masterplan for the town. The new ‘Eco Hub’ will provide improved space for the community activities that already run from the building, which revolve around learning, creativity, food and families. New facilities will also be added, including a resource hub, low carbon meeting space and energy efficient units for a broad range of community activities It will continue to house Salto Arts, the organisation behind the Whitley Bay Carnival. Advertisement Hide Ad Sarah Sutton, community engagement worker with Whitley Bay Big Local, added: “The refurbishment means that we can really develop the space to continue the great work we already do in the community. “The space will still be as welcoming, but more flexible to suit the needs of a wider range of community members while really looking towards having less of a negative impact on our environment. “We will soon be commissioning an architect to work with our building users and us to design a modern, green and inventive place for the community to belong, meet, learn and do.” Ian Smith, the former owner of the building, said: "I am delighted that Big Local has now purchased our family building on Whitley Road. Advertisement Hide Ad “We have owned this building for over 40 years, firstly as a printing works and then as the Job Centre. “All of the family, who are still local, are over the moon that this building will now be used for the benefit of the local community. We feel it will be a great asset to the town and a wonderful centre point for the local people.” Whitley Bay Big Local will be consulting the public on what they want from the new building at two events – its AGM on November 20, and the Whitley Bay Fiesta on Sunday, September 5. For more information on the events, and the charity, visit www.facebook.com/WhitleyBayBigLocal and www.whitleybaybiglocal.org.uk

Document: Whetstone Community Hub   Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png A vision for an inclusive community  space and a hub of disability support  in the heart of  Blaby District. To mark our 125th year, we are excited to announce our vision to create an all-encompassing hub for the local disabled community in the heart of Blaby District in Whetstone, Leicestershire. Embarking on this project on the 125th anniversary of the charity aims to act as a catalyst to further our work in enhancing the lives of disabled people by creating spaces where disabled communities can thrive. ​ The three buildings on site promise to become an all-encompassing hub for the local disabled community in the heart of Blaby in Whetstone, Leicestershire. Here are just some of the ways the chapel, community buildings and the manse are set to enhance the lives of disabled people. Get Involved Donate Care. Support. Community. The Chapel A vibrant space for community, engagement, involvement, and support. We want to create a place where everyone is welcome, a place of community spirit, work, and volunteering opportunities, and above all else positive impact for all. ​ Some of the events and services to run from the chapel include: Exhibitions and Events Information Services Outreach Projects ​Support Groups ​ Vocational Training The location of the Whetstone Baptist Church is particularly significant, as it has been a staple in the community for many years. We're committed to preserving the historic nature of the building while modernising it to meet the needs of disabled people and the wider community. ​ Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Together, we can do so much. The Community Buildings We'll be working tirelessly to transform the community buildings into truly accessible and equipped spaces, complete with sensory rooms and changing places, for the delivery of care and support services for disabled people. ​ The buildings will compliment our Head Office and become another home to Mosaic 1898's Adult Day Services and Children's groups. ​ The versatility of the space excitingly allows us to engage with the local community, schools, and businesses to do so much more - a hub of social good. ​ There's no place like home. The Manse An around the clock respite care facility providing sought after non-nursing short breaks for disabled people with complex needs. This purposeful provision will offer Leicestershire families and caregivers the support and means to take respite with peace of mind that their loved ones are well cared for. ​ We know first hand how much a place like this is needed in Leicestershire. A home away from home. ​ We're excited by the road ahead and we'd love to invite you to join us and be a part of the journey. Get in touch with us if you'd like to get involved in any way. Contact Us Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Thank you to our Funders. ​ We are incredibly grateful to our funders for supporting the Whetstone Hub Project at Mosaic 1898. Their generosity is more than financial, it’s a powerful investment in inclusion, community, and lasting change. Together, we're building a more accessible, connected, and compassionate world. Thank you for being part of this journey.   Whetstone Baptist Church Flooring Installation – Whetstone Baptist Church (New Build Project) We were proud to be involved in the new build of Whetstone Baptist Church, supplying and installing all floor coverings throughout the purpose-built facility. This exciting project gave us the opportunity to contribute to the creation of a welcoming, modern environment for worship, community events, and day-to-day church activities. From the early planning stages, we worked closely with the church and the main contractor to specify flooring solutions that would meet the demands of a busy, multi-use building. The brief required a combination of aesthetics, durability, and ease of maintenance — all while creating a warm and inviting atmosphere for visitors of all ages. We supplied and installed a range of high-performance products, including: Commercial-grade carpet tiles in the main worship space and meeting rooms, offering comfort underfoot and acoustic benefits. Safety vinyl flooring in circulation areas, entrances, and washrooms to ensure slip resistance and longevity. Durable vinyl wood-effect flooring in multifunctional spaces for a clean, modern finish. Our team carried out the full installation across the building, ensuring everything was delivered on time and to the highest standard in coordination with other trades on site. The result is a beautifully finished space, ready to serve the community for years to come. It was a pleasure to be part of a project that brings so much value to the local area, and we’re honoured to have contributed to such a meaningful new chapter for Whetstone Baptist Church.

Document: The Heart   Protecting the Heart of Newhaven Published: 24 Aug 2023 Updated: 25 Aug 2023 5 minute read Gala Day 270523 Andrew Perry 213 Content by Share Sign up for email updates Print this page Giving a new life to Edinburgh’s oldest working school When it became clear the Heart of Newhaven, Edinburgh’s oldest working primary school, could no longer accommodate the number of pupils in the area, the community feared the building would fall into private hands. Situated within a conservation area, the iconic main building, built in 1844, is a local landmark. Grade B listed and part of the original Newhaven village, its cultural heritage and connections to the community go back to the area’s roots in fishing. In 2020 the Heart of Newhaven Community SCIO was formed and undertook a successful Community Asset Transfer on behalf of the community. Funding to buy the school was then secured from The Scottish Land Fund. Saving the buildings ensured they will continue as a much-needed community space in an area where these types of spaces are in short supply. Making a difference to the community Following the successful transfer of the building, the community has wasted no time in establishing a wide range of activities at the Heart. Their focus is on delivering projects across three main themes of ‘Culture & Heritage’, ‘Learning and Enterprise’ and ‘Health and Wellbeing’. Sitting across these is an overarching commitment to intergenerational work, bringing people together and keeping a connection to the previous use of the building as a school. The community hub that is now run from the Heart offers spaces for theatre and dance groups, choir and music groups, sport and performances of all kinds, artists’ studios, community meeting rooms for rent, a Victorian School Room, run by partner the History of Education Centre, The Wee Museum with displays of local heritage, Dementia meeting place, Pregnancy café, community teaching kitchen, much needed early years’ provision, a Community Garden and The Creel (a portacabin) for the Men’s Shed to carry on their activities. Situated within a conservation area, the iconic main building, built in 1844, is a local landmark. Grade B listed and part of the original Newhaven village, its cultural heritage and connections to the community go back to the area’s roots in fishing. Gala Day 270523 Andrew Perry 188 Funding the project Heart of Newhaven was awarded £300,000 from the Community Ownership Fund - £250,000 in capital and £50,00 of revenue ‘to deliver the restoration and renovation of a former primary school for community use’. The grant needs to be matched with equivalent funding and work continues to secure further amounts. Getting the right support A number of organisations assisted with essential support and advice as the project was developed. The Community Ownership Support Service provided guidance throughout the asset transfer, helping to navigate the complexities involved with this process. The Scottish Land Fund advisor was important in helping with the early stages of the project and guiding the community through the acquisition of the building. Architectural Heritage Fund provided funding and support to meet architect fees. A local organisation, Pilmeny Development Project, was instrumental in supporting the Heart to become established, advising on developing intergenerational projects. They remain a key delivery partner. Important partnerships are now being developed with other organisations in the area. Heart of Newhaven Community are part of Restoration Forth, led by World Wildlife Fund with The Heart being one of 6 community hubs around the Firth of Forth aiming to re-introduce seagrass and oysters. In addition, working with a range of public and third sector partners on primary care development ensures the Heart contributes to a strategic approach to wellbeing in Newhaven. Final thoughts Considering and planning all your match funders requirements and deadlines is essential to ensure funding isn’t lost Volunteer-led projects are challenging for those involved, a considerable level of knowledge and time is required to interact with what can be challenging processes. Bring in paid staff or make use of expert advisors whenever possible Well evidenced community consultation is essential for any project – get it done properly and at an early stage

Document: Centre for Social Enterprise and Creativity   Case study Centre for social enterprise in Bishop Auckland receives £65,000 A former community centre in Bishop Auckland is being transformed into a new social enterprise and creativity hub. The project is supported with over £65,000 from the Community Ownership Fund. From: Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities Published 17 January 2023 Dorset Place Community Centre The new centre Dorset Place Community Centre first opened in the 1970s but has been derelict for several years. Bridge Creative, a community interest company, is leading the project. The new centre will: offer support for adults with learning disabilities and autism across County Durham include enterprise spaces for local social enterprises and community organisations Opportunities for the local community People from the local community have been involved in the creation of the new centre. Ben Tinkler, Director at Bridge Creative said, The funding has enabled us to complete our community-led project.  In true community spirit, 12 adults with learning disabilities and autistic adults have worked alongside the contractors to decorate the centre, gaining real work experience and learning a new trade, working on a centre that they will benefit from for years to come! We can’t wait to see the benefits the Centre of Social Enterprise is going to have on the local community. Find out more about levelling up. Published 17 January 2023   Community Centre on Henknowle to be transformed By Bishop Press -March 25, 2022 Artist impression of the exterior of Dorset Place Community Centre A former community centre near the heart of Bishop Auckland is being transformed into a social enterprise and creativity hub that supports adults with learning disabilities and autism. Dorset Place Community Centre, located on Henknowle Estate, is in the process of transformed with over £260,000 investment by Bridge Creative. In November 2021, believe housing transferred ownership of the building to provide a base for Bridge Creative and bring the building back into a community use. Bridge Creative was founded in April 2018 and are a community interest company (CIC) who have been searching for a home in the area for three years. The organisation already supports around 160 people in County Durham, the new premises will allow them to increase their offer in the south of the county. Bridge Creative secured a total of £261,245 funding for the refurbishment of the centre with money from South Durham CLLD through the European Structural Investment Fund Programme 2014-2020, the North East Local Enterprise Partnership (LEP), The Gaunless Gateway Big Local Partnership, The Screwfix Foundation, Power to Change Bright Idea’s Fund and The School for Social Entrepreneurs. Once completed, the new centre will provide a permanent base for Bridge Creative to continue its employment support for adults with learning disabilities and autism, offering work experience opportunities and supporting people into paid employment in the events, arts and creative industries. Artist impression on how the interior will look once completed. In their new home Bridge Creative will support 10 young adults with learning disabilities and or autism to complete a traineeship as part of the refurbishment, working alongside contractors and tradespeople to renovate the building, gaining real work experience and a nationally-recognised qualification. The centre will also include five affordable spaces for local social enterprises, charitable organisations, and creative businesses, as well as a commercial kitchen, and co-working space with individual pods. Fay Metha from believe housing said: “Bridge Creative do some great work across County Durham, when they approached us during their search for a base we were determined to see if we could find a solution. “The building at Dorset Place has been unused for a while so transferring ownership to Bridge Creative is a great way of bringing it back into community use. It will also provide a brilliant new resource for the community that I’m sure will be really well used.” Excited Ben Tinkler, Managing Director of Bridge Creative said: “We’re very excited that we now have the keys and work started on our new building in January. “It’ll be great for the local area and for the people we support and will provide many opportunities for people with learning disabilities and autism to access meaningful paid employment. “Homeworking is also expected to continue beyond the pandemic so we think the co-working space will be particularly popular with people wanting to work from somewhere other than their bedroom. It’s took almost two years to get to this stage so we just can’t wait to get started.” Physical works have already started and will include accessibility improvements including widened doors for wheelchair users and the installation of a lift to make the first floor accessible. The aim is to offer affordable and flexible space that will benefit the local community and provide opportunities for the people Bridge Creative supports.   Welcome to THE COSE - CENTRE OF SOCIAL ENTERPRISE Building A Dream Back in November 2021, we bought a building in Bishop Auckland to become our new base. The building had been unused for a long time meaning it needed a lot of refurbishment work. Doing Good All Round We supported 12 adults with learning disabilities to complete a traineeship and gain work experience on the project, working with Trades4Care CIC on the painting and decorating of the centre. All Together Now The COSE (pronounced co-zee) offers a base for our projects and services, and will be home to a number of local social enterprises and community organisations. The COSE also offers affordable, creative Co-Working space and space for local adults with learning disabilities and autistic adults to set up their own social enterprise. Making A Start January 2022 marked the beginning of the refurbishment! Once complete, we'll have a bright and accessible community space with "living wall", affordable social enterprise space, art installations and much more! We ran Design Thinking workshops with the people we support to come up with the design and layout of the centre. Have a look at our "visions for the future" below to see some of the exciting plans for the centre! Finally there! In August 2022 we finally moved into our new home! We're over the moon to finally have a permanent base and we can't wait to see the huge positive impact The COSE is going to have on the people we support and the local community for years to come! For Space and Room bookings, contact admin@bridgecreative.org. Thank You! We secured £327,115 funding for the refurbishment of the centre, with huge thanks to our incredible funders for making this project possible and seeing the value in our vision! South Durham CLLD through the European Structural Investment Fund Programme 2014-2020, The Community Ownership Fund, the North East Local Enterprise Partnership (LEP), The Gaunless Gateway Big Local Partnership, The Screwfix Foundation, Power to Change Bright Idea’s Fund and The School for Social Entrepreneurs

Document: Hudswell Hostel @ St Michaels   Rishi Visits Newly Opened Walkers’ Hostel in Hudswell Friday, 6 June, 2025 Local News Rishi Sunak at St Michael's Lodge in Hudswell Rishi visited the newly completed St Michael’s Lodge in Hudswell, a 16-bed walkers’ hostel developed by the Hudswell Community Charity. The hostel has been created through the conversion of the village’s historic St Michael’s Church, part funded by the UK Shared Prosperity Fund and the Community Ownership Fund – both established by Rishi Sunak in his time as Chancellor. During this visit, the Rishi was shown around the new facilities – including a lounge, meeting room, and kitchen by Melanie Sadler, the hostel Manager and Martin Booth, Secretary of Hudswell Community Charity. Rishi also met local residents and trustees to hear about the vision behind the project and its anticipated benefits for the area. The visit ended with Rishi signing the visitors book, as the first visitor to the completed site. The project builds on the charity’s successful track record in delivering community benefits, including the management of affordable housing in the village, such as the cottages Rishi visited in January. St Michaels Lodge was officially opened on the 25th May by Yorkshire Vet, Julian Norton, in a village event which boasted over 200 attendees who joined to celebrate the opening. The Hostel is now open for bookings, via their website - stmichaelslodge.com Speaking after the visit, Rishi said: “This is a fantastic example of community spirit and determination delivering something truly special for our area. St Michael’s Lodge will not only provide a warm welcome for walkers and visitors, but also support the local economy and help sustain rural life. I’m proud to have supported the project and the brilliant volunteers behind it.” He added: “Community-led projects like this are vital for the future of rural North Yorkshire. I’ll continue to champion and support them wherever possible.” Martin Booth said: “We were delighted to show Rishi around St Michael's Lodge and pleased to see how impressed he was with what we have created. He has supported the project right from the start and visited us before any work began, so he has a full appreciation of transformation of the church to the Lodge. “

Document: Lascelles Hall CC Community Clubhouse   Lascelles Hall Community Clubhouse Completion Huddersfield, West Yorkshire, United Kingdom Lascelles Hall Community Clubhouse Completion £31,285 raised so far 59 supporters Donate Share Crowdfunder is secure Your payment details are protected Richard Marshall About the project owner Always on This project successfully funded on 10th September 2025, you can still support them with a donation. Aim: It's 15 years since the arson attack on our treasured community clubhouse. The rebuild is done but require £30,000 to complete the interior. The ask and who we are Thanks to Sport England, if we raise £15,000, Sport England will match it, unlocking an incredible £30,000 to complete the interior of our clubhouse.  We are celebrating our 200th birthday this year, Huddersfield's oldest and one of the oldest in Yorkshire, a remarkable legacy and the club and community are proud of - help us make this a special year! Lascelles Hall Cricket Club has been in the heart of the village of Lascelles Hall since 1825.  Lascelles Hall Cricket Club has brought many happy memories of community life and cricket to the village. However, the club suffered a bleak period from 2010 when our historical and treasured clubhouse was destroyed by fire in an arson attack. A temporary ‘portacabin style’ clubhouse was erected and has functioned effectively since this date although not sustainable for another 200 years!  In our 200th year, we have many events planned including a bicentennial game against a ex representative Yorkshire side on 13th July and an MCC side on the 24th August.The club is quite simply the focal point of the community, in fact there is no other public or community building in the village. It is a hub to strengthen village community life and bring people together in many different ways. 1748265879_our_club.jpg Why are we crowdfunding? For over a decade, we’ve worked to secure funding to rebuild the only community building in our village. In 2024, we were awarded £300,000 from the Community Ownership Fund to construct the exterior of a new clubhouse. In addition, we have raised other funding and used savings to complete all the exterior and landscaping areas totalling over £350,000.  However, we are facing a funding gap of £30,000 for the interior fit out and are seeking support to ensure the building is fully inclusive and accessible. How we’ll spend the money raised We have had incredible support from Easy Bathroom who generously provided all the shower/sanitary requirements (Over £17k) and Vivly Living who supplied a kitchen and internal doors (Over £20k) but require professional support from specific trades to fit out the internal requirements.  This includes plumbing, electrical fix, wall, floor and ceiling finishes.  We have an army of volunteers but need specific specialist help in these areas.  The building has been fitted with an air source heat pump complete with underfloor heating providing a sustainable solution for generations to enjoy all year around. Community impact Our rural, small village has no community centre/space. Lascelles Hall is in the bottom quarter nationally for access to services due to no community infrastructure being available. Our club has always filled this void but since the arson attack we haven't been able to adequately support our community with a base for voting/training-events/community-groups/social-events/healthy-pastimes. Our new clubhouse is a good step forward but this project is vital to ensure everyone in the community can access our facilities and natural/open green space. We have surveyed the local community as part of our new facility development, receiving 48 responses. Each respondent answered 12 questions, with unanimous support for the new clubhouse. 1748266179_200_crest_v1.png How you can play your part Your support can make twice the difference - but we need to hit that target to unlock the full amount!  Every donation, no matter the size, brings us closer to our goal.  Whether you are a lifelong cricket fan, a local business or someone passionate about community projects, your contribution will make a real difference.  There are four simple ways to help us reach our target, and make this project a reality. 1. Make a pledge. Don’t delay if you’re going to support us because momentum is key to our success! 2. Spread the word. Share our project on your social media pages and tell the world to get behind us! The more people we reach, the more support we will get. 3. Offer rewards. Get involved if you are a local business by donating things we can offer to our supporters. We will give you a shout out! 4. Fundraise for us. If you want to run your own fundraiser – maybe a sponsored activity or similar – use the 'contact project' button at the top of our page to let us know. Setting up your own page only takes a few minutes and you won’t need any bank details. Just a fun idea!   Lascelles Hall Cricket Club – 200th Anniversary Celebration Match 10 Jul 2025 Join Lascelles Hall Cricket Club this weekend for a truly historic day as they celebrate its 200th anniversary with a special commemorative fixture against Yorkshire County Cricket Club. Sunday, 13th July | Lascelles Hall Cricket Club Gates Open: 1:00 PM | First Ball: 2:00 PM This match continues a proud tradition, recreating the historic encounter held on 10th July 1975, when Yorkshire played Lascelles Hall to mark the club’s 150th anniversary. Fifty years later, the celebration continues – this time with the opening of our brand-new clubhouse (above), with the function room, bar and patio in use. Featuring Confirmed Yorkshire CCC Players: Jonny Tattersall, Finlay Bean, Ben Cliff, Noah Kelly, Yash Vagadia, Chris Schofield, Alex Morris, and Jawad Akhtar Find out more on:

Document: The reopening of Whitworth Leisure Centre Whitworth Leisure CIC supported by new Levelling Up cash 24th March 2024 National government By Natalie Horrobin Trainee Digital Reporter northernjourno Share 0 comments Whitworth Leisure Centre (Image: Robbie McDonald) Whitworth Leisure Centre (Image: Robbie McDonald) An East Lancashire leisure centre is one of seven that has been handed cash by the Government to help it stay open and serving the community. Whitworth Leisure Centre is one of the centres sharing £2.4 million in investment unveiled by the Department for Levelling Up today, through its Community Ownership Fund. The cash is designed to protect facilities run by the community to ensure they can continue to serve local people. In December, ownership of the John Street facility was transferred by Rossendale Council to the new Whitworth Leisure Community Interest Company. The council had announced plans to close the centre as part of cost-cutting measures, sparking anger and protests at the decision in the town. The leisure centre reopened last month after being taken over by the community. Whitworth Leisure CIC has now been handed £216,000 by the Government to support the reopening of the centre. The CIC said this investment means the centre's future is "brighter than ever before". In a statement, it said: "At the beginning of our fight to save Whitworth Leisure Centre from closure we knew the Community Ownership Fund was the key to success. "The delays in returning the ownership of the building to the community meant we couldn’t apply until the beginning of this year, but despite the hurdles and delays we persisted nonetheless. "The rigorous application process demanded extensive effort and commitment, underscoring the resilience of the team behind saving Whitworth Leisure Centre, specifically the relentless perseverance of Alan Billingsley and Jo Furtado. "Without the faith in the CIC from Whitworth Town Council and Mayor Mike Royds this achievement would not have been possible. The funding will see the centre's transformation become a reality, ensuring it remains a vital asset for the community for years to come. "The secured funding will facilitate a meticulous refurbishment of the leisure centre, breathing new life into its facilities. "Key enhancements include essential repairs and maintenance for the large pool and building, the renewal of changing facilities, as well as the creation of an innovative multi-use studio space, making the centre safe and welcoming and able to continue to provide accessible health, leisure and swimming facilities in Whitworth." Across the country, more than 80 projects are sharing £33.5 million in funding to keep serving their communities. Ruth Hempsall Jones, from Whitworth Leisure CIC, said: "We are immensely grateful for the overwhelming support received from the community, all of our volunteers, and every person involved in this journey so far. "The fundraising from each individual, businesses and our members has been nothing short of incredible. "This achievement is a testament to what can be accomplished when a community comes together to save community facilities.” Jacob Young, minister for levelling up, said:  “We know how much these vital community assets mean to people across the country. "They are an important lifeline for people young and old, and they’re the beating heart of our towns, cities and villages. “That’s why we’re stepping in to protect them with a major rescue package, so we stop these great establishments closing or being lost forever and ensure that they continue to sit at the heart of our beloved communities.” From Monday, community-owned groups can apply for more funding in the final round of grants from the Community Ownership Fund. The first bidding window opens on Monday, March 25, and closes on Wednesday, April 10. Swimming pool to reopen after long campaign A view of Whitworth Leisure Centre from the car park Image source,LDRS Image caption, Whitworth Leisure Centre was built in the 1970s ByRobbie Macdonald Local Democracy Reporting Service Published 21 July 2024 A swimming pool is due to reopen to the public after being saved by campaigners. Whitworth Leisure Centre in Rossendale, Lancashire, was closed last year by the council amid financial concerns, prompting months of protests. Volunteers formed the Whitworth Leisure Centre Community Interest Company and took over the running of the centre after raising funds. The centre, which dates from the 1970s, includes a pool and gym facilities. Two children hold up a 'Save Our Pool' sign Image source,LDRS Image caption, The closure promoted protests across the community An update on the pool was given at the last meeting of Rossendale Council. Councillor Alan Neal said: “The swimming pool has been refilled and it is not leaking. "The hope is to reopen at the end of August. It’s a credit to the hard work by everyone.” The centre was closed by the council amid finance worries but the decision sparked outrage. The council said rising inflation and operating costs had forced them to act. But campaigners said the pool was an essential resource for both the young and old, including school children’s swimming lessons.

This will benefit the local community and sports groups who currently use the facilities. From: Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities Published 17 January 2023 A thriving hub in north Doncaster Local community groups aim to refresh the facilities. The plans include: new changing rooms improved pitches a new pavilion Find out more about levelling up. Published 17 January 2023   Adwick Park Community Pavilion By Adwick Park Community and Sports Academies CIO  Adwick le Street  Delivery Adwick Park Community Pavilion Like 1  Follow 35 Our charity aims to replace tired old porta cabins and build a new community sports pavilon hosting a club house and suitable changing facilities for cricket and football. funded iconFunded on 09 Aug 2020! 39 backers £10,658 raised Adwick Park Community and Sports Academies CIO About me Adwick Park Community and Sports Academies CIO Idea Gallery Costs Backers Volunteers Comments Our new charity Adwick Park Community and Sports Academies has been set up to bring people together. Our dream is to open a community hub in Adwick Park where we will run sports clubs amongst other community-led groups such as a youth clubs and social events. Without a community building, there is a real possibility that both cricket and football teams playing at Adwick Park will not continue beyond the 2021 season which wil be Adwick Le Street CC centenary season and Adwick Park Rangers JFC 15-year anniversary. We have been granted full planning permission (December 2019) and we have secured significant funds (approximately 85% of our funding goal) to pay for the building materials and labour. We are appealing to businesses and anyone with a local connection to Adwick Park to help fundraise the remaining amount. By supporting the project you will be leaving a pledge and when we reach our fundraising goal will become a donation to our charity. Many thanks for your support. What we'll deliver: Detailed drawings of the community pavilion Why it's a great idea: The outline building designs approved on the 17th December 2019 are reasonably sized (c.700m2), but unobtrusive, well-designed building that adds to the quality of the park. The building encompasses changing facilities as well as a relatively large 'function' space that give ideal opportunities such as social and meeting space especially during evenings when the local library is closed. The existing porta cabin units are not thought to have enough life span left within them to act as a foundation to warrant enhancement/development and major investment. The intended functions of the community pavilion will be used for the following means: • Meeting venue for organised events • Training courses for residents • Changing facilities for participants for sports teams • Toilet facilities for spectators and participants • Officiating and scoring • Cafe and bar facilities Steps to get it done: Detailed costs of build ready for tender notice forming plans into detailed layouts A tender notice of local contractors will be invited to tender for all works. Designs and tender quotations will be cost checked by appointed project manager. A pre-start meeting will be arranged involving the contractor and the landowner. Work will commence on site by approved contractor. Practical completion of works undertaken by contractor. The community pavilion will: - Provide the residents of Adwick and the local communities of North Doncaster with a thriving community run facility for sport, leisure, social activities. - Advance residents health and well-being and promote a stronger sense of community. - Offer volunteering, work experience and sports coaching opportunities to local people, especially young people. - Involve community hub participants and residents in running the facility and pursuing ways of sustaining the community pavilion. Document: Redevelopment of closed tennis courts into a Multi Use Games Area (MUGA)   £288K to revamp village tennis courts Sport Dec 29, 2023 by Sebastian Salcedo Linked In X Facebook bookmark_border Tarbert has received a massive sporting boost with a £288,577 grant which will bring the tennis courts back to life and provide a new multi-use games area. The dilapidated courts at Tarbert Bowling Club have been closed over 20 years. But this significant grant will fix the drainage, provide floodlighting and new fencing. This will create a space for community members to play a range of sports, with free weekly sessions for the village's school. One of the courts will be used for tennis while the other will be marked out with lines for a variety of sports. The grant comes from the UK Government’s Community Ownership Fund, a funding pot rescuing community places that form the beating heart of a local community. This funding makes up 80 per cent of the cost of repairing the courts, with the remaining 20 per cent to be raised locally. The bowling club now has one year to find contractors and get the work done. And once the sports courts are up and running, the next stage of the project will see the renovation of the bowling club and provision of new changing facilities. Duncan MacGregor, president of Tarbert Bowling Club, said: “We are very grateful to Tarbert Harbour Board which has given us a great deal of help filling out the forms. “The bowling club committee put out questionnaires and the response was really good. “The games area will cater for a variety of sports including basketball and will be used by several organisations, groups and individuals. There will be disabled access and it will be suitable for all ages and abilities. “We have got another meeting coming up to form a sports council in Tarbert. Between the bowling club and sports council we can take it forward. Bookings will be done through that. “We are hoping to get floodlighting so that it can be used in the evenings all year round.” He added: “Everyone at the bowling club is over the moon to receive this funding. I would like to say a special thanks for all the hard work to Tarbert Harbour Board and the bowling club committee. “This will be a great asset to the village and will obviously be good for people’s fitness and mental wellbeing. It will also help to support tourism. “We have had a lot of younger members join the bowling club in the last few years, and a younger committee pushing for sporting facilities in Tarbert, which is great.” The funding is part of an £8million package to help protect Scotland’s most valued community places. UK Government Minister for Scotland Malcolm Offord said: "It's great news that a further 17 Scottish projects are sharing £8million from the UK Government Community Ownership Fund. "Through the fund we are now supporting 43 community groups across Scotland to the tune of £13.6 million to breathe new life into the places where they live, work and play. “In total we are directly investing more than £2.7 billion in hundreds of projects across Scotland as we help to grow our economy and level up the country." Secretary of State for Levelling Up Michael Gove added: “Investing in communities improves lives – whether that’s rebuilding a community centre, rescuing historic pubs and theatres or upgrading sports facilities used by families every day. “These are unique and cherished places, valued by local people of all ages, and we know how much they mean to communities. The Community Ownership Fund is about restoring pride in the places people call home and I’m delighted to be funding these projects.” The UK fund – worth a total £150 million - was launched in 2021 to provide targeted support for community places that most impact local people. Document: York Supplies Community Co-op   York Supplies Community Society was created by a large group of diverse local people who love York Supplies and want to ensure it continues to serve our local community as it has since 1947. In 2022, a significant number of customers of York Supplies expressed concern that the shop might be lost to the community upon the retirement of Jon and Jean Jaffa, the respected owners of the business since 1986. A series of meetings were held in local venues and it was clear that there was enormous support for action to continue the business. Customers were invited to an awayday to explore options and more than 20 came and it was decided that a community benefit society would be the best way of acquiring and running the business. This was not a minor undertaking.  Following the awayday, interested customers were invited to come on to a committee of the York Supplies Action Group, which became the acting committee of the new Society. Following an application to the Financial Conduct Authority, a rigorous process which included background checks on all of the committee, approval was obtained to create York Supplies Community Society Limited. The Society launched a highly successful community share offer, and after a testing process was granted matching funding from the Government’s Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.  These funds enabled us to acquire York Supplies and begin to operate it on behalf of the local community. The initial acting directors stepped down at the Society’s first annual general meeting of members on 11 July 2024.  Some stood for re-election, with others making way after their contribution over the last 18 months.   Community raises £350k to save Birmingham hardware shop Published 24 October 2023 Share Part of the community group with West Midlands mayor Andy Street Image source,Andy Street Image caption, A community benefit society has been formed to run the hardware and garden shop Vanessa Pearce BBC News, West Midlands A community has raised more than £350,000 in six weeks to save a much-loved hardware and garden shop. York Supplies has been trading in the Kings Heath area of Birmingham for 75 years. A community share offer inviting local residents to invest in the business and form a co-operative had been fully subscribed, organisers said. It was the first time in England a hardware shop had been saved in this way, the group claimed. Matching funds from the government had enabled the group to move quickly to begin the purchase of the shop, it added. York Supplies Image caption, York Supplies has served Kings Heath customers for 75 years The store's current owner Jon Jaffa, who has had the shop for 35 years, gave campaigners first refusal and time to raise money. He said he was "thrilled the business has been saved for another generation". "York Supplies has always been a central part of the Kings Heath community," he added. The Waterloo Road shop was founded in 1947 and has only had three sets of owners. It counts The Beatles as former customers who reportedly called in when they were playing at a nearby dance hall in the 1960s. Jon Jaffa Image caption, Current owner Jon Jaffa gave campaigners first refusal to buy the store Naomi Standen, chair of York Supplies Action Group, said members had been "astounded and delighted" by the level of support. "We always believed there was a lot of love for York Supplies in the community," she explained. The group received approval from the Financial Conduct Authority to establish a community benefit society, called York Supplies Community Society Ltd, which means profits must be used for community purposes. The campaign had received the backing of West Midlands mayor Andy Street, who said its success was "wonderful".

Life Dronfield, a non-profit organisation, has been working for over a decade to open a new community hub in the town and, this week, made a breakthrough with their successful bid into the Government’s Community Ownership Fund. The funds will allow the charity to fully refurbish the Parish Halls building, which they secured a few years ago on behalf of the local community. The charity hopes to use the building as a new community hub for new parents, families, young people after school, and older people to meet and socialise.
